content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 18, 1993

Larry Pistoresi, Jr.
Greenhills Holdings
1128 W. Robertson Blvd.
Chowchilla, CA 93610

Re: Your Request for Assistance
Our Advice File No. I-93-401

Dear Mr. Pistoresi:

This is in response to your request for assistance under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/
Since you are not seeking a specific decision,
your request is considered a request for informal assistance.2/

QUESTION

Does your wife's position on the Chowchilla City Council
prohibit you from making presentations on land use applications
before the city council?

CONCLUSION

Your wife's position on the city council does not prohibit
you from making a presentation to the city council on a land use
entitlement decision. However, your spouse is prohibited from
participating in any decision which will have a material financial
effect on your employer.

FACTS

Your spouse was recently elected to the Chowchilla City
Council. You are a salaried employee of a land development
company. The company processes land-use entitlement applications

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Advice File No. I-93-401
Page 2


before the city.  As the project director for the company, you
make formal presentations before the city council.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official's immediate
family,3/ or on:

* * *

(c)  Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

"Income" as used above, means a payment received, including
but not limited to any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest,
rent, proceeds from any sale, or gift.  Income also includes any
community property interest in the income of a spouse.  (Section
82030.)

Your spouse has a community property interest in the income
you receive as salary from your employer.  Accordingly, as a
public official, she may not make, participate in making, or
attempt to use her official position to influence a governmental
decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable4/


3/  An official's "immediate family" includes his spouse and
dependent children.  (Section 82029.)

4/  Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.  An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.  Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.  (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Advice File No. I-93-401
Page 3


material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally, on your employer. (Section 87103(c).)

Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official's economic interest in a decision is "material" as required by Section 87103. If an official's
interest is directly involved in the decision, then Regulation
18702.1 applies to determine materiality. If the interest is
indirectly involved, then Regulation 18702.2 applies. Since there
is no specific decision before the city council at this time,
there is no need to discuss which materiality regulation should be
applied. However, I have enclosed both Regulation 18702.1 and
18702.2 to be used in the future to determine when a decision will
have a material financial effect on your employer.6/

As a member of the immediate family of a public official, you
are not subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibiting public
officials from using their official position to influence a
governmental decision only apply to the public official and do not
extend to members of the official's immediate family.

I trust this answers your question sufficiently. Should your
spouse have questions concerning her duties under the provisions
of the Act, she should contact the Commission's Legal Division at
(916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel,

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

SGC/JET/jt
Enclosures


5/ A source of income is directly involved in a decision when it
initiates a proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal, or
similar request or is a named party in, or is the subject of the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)

6/ For your guidance, I have also enclosed advice letters to
public officials which address participation in governmental
decisions which may have a material financial effect on a source
of income.

However,
